hero.title: Interface studies from a frontend engineer sharpening design craft.
hero.description: These Figma studies sit alongside the portfolio rebuild and document how I am strengthening typography, layout, systems thinking, and motion design in public.

intro.title: Why this work is here now
intro.description: I’m deliberately building the design side of my practice in public. These studies document what I am testing, what I am learning, and how that learning feeds back into the way I build products.

items.title: Editorial storytelling
items.description: Learning how typography, pacing, and composition can carry technical credibility without making the interface feel dense or dry.

items.title: System thinking in visual form
items.description: Exploring how one visual language can support project cards, case studies, writing surfaces, and design galleries without losing clarity.

items.title: Motion-ready interface structure
items.description: Designing screens with enough rhythm and hierarchy that future motion can clarify transitions and focus rather than compensate for weak layout.

collection.title: The first five portfolio studies
collection.description: Each direction tackles a different portfolio problem: first impression, project browsing, long-form case studies, writing, and how to show design work in public.

designDetailPage.reflectionTitle: What this study is teaching me

portfolio-home-direction.label: Portfolio Redesign / Home
portfolio-home-direction.cardCaption: Hero hierarchy, proof signals, and a first impression that feels deliberate without becoming loud.

hero.title: Designing a homepage that feels credible before it feels flashy.
hero.description: This direction explores how to present my frontend depth clearly while making room for the design practice I am deliberately building.

portfolio-home-direction.summary: A homepage direction built around credibility, clear proof of depth, and enough visual character to feel considered.
overview: The homepage needed to do two jobs at once. It had to establish credibility quickly for recruiters and hiring managers, and it also had to hint at a more design-conscious direction than a typical frontend portfolio.
This study focuses on the opening rhythm: strong type, evidence of systems work, a visible design taste, and a structure that can evolve when more design-led projects start joining the portfolio.

principles.title: Credibility up front
principles.description: The first screen should make experience, specialization, and proof visible before asking visitors to scroll for validation.

principles.title: Editorial restraint
principles.description: The layout avoids spectacle-led interaction and instead relies on proportion, typography, and spacing to create presence.

principles.title: Room to grow
principles.description: The structure leaves space for the future Design Engineer positioning without requiring a future rewrite of the entire homepage.

gallery.title: Homepage direction
gallery.caption: The selected screen leans on type, proof markers, and measured contrast to create a precise first impression.

portfolio-home-direction.reflection: This study is teaching me that strong first impressions come more from hierarchy and restraint than from novelty. Once the structure is confident, the rest can stay subtle.

selected-work-archive.label: Portfolio Redesign / Work Index
selected-work-archive.cardCaption: A catalogue system for engineering work that keeps narrative quality instead of collapsing into plain thumbnails.

hero.title: Turning project depth into a browseable archive.
hero.description: This direction explores how to show a broad range of projects without flattening everything into the same kind of card.

selected-work-archive.summary: A selected-work listing that gives each project enough context to feel intentional, not just listed.
overview: The work index has to carry a diverse set of projects: design systems, AI product work, full-stack commerce, and open source experiments. The challenge is making that range feel coherent rather than scattered.
This study creates a visual system that can separate shipped product work from exploratory work while keeping both within one deliberate editorial tone.

principles.title: Fast scanning with real context
principles.description: Each card should expose enough role, scope, and relevance that the visitor can decide what to open without guessing.

principles.title: Clear grouping
principles.description: Professional delivery and experimental work need different framing so the archive feels curated rather than mixed together by accident.

principles.title: Reusable card language
principles.description: The visual rules for cards, labels, and imagery should scale cleanly as more projects and prototypes are added over time.

gallery.title: Selected work archive
gallery.caption: The screen is designed to support scanning, comparison, and progression into deeper case-study reading.

selected-work-archive.reflection: This study is helping me think more carefully about browse surfaces. The important question is not how much to show, but what deserves emphasis when someone is deciding where to click.

case-study-storytelling.label: Portfolio Redesign / Case Study
case-study-storytelling.cardCaption: Long-form storytelling for contribution, technical decisions, and product impact without turning into a résumé dump.

hero.title: Designing a case-study page that can carry real engineering depth.
hero.description: This direction explores how to combine narrative, screenshots, code context, and implementation detail without making the page feel heavy.

case-study-storytelling.summary: A case-study template for explaining context, constraints, implementation, and contribution without turning the page into a resume dump.
overview: The portfolio needs project pages where I can express not just what was built, but what constraints shaped the work, what decisions I made, and where my contribution changed the outcome.
This study introduces an editorial structure with a persistent side rail, long-form reading rhythm, and room for both visual evidence and code-grounded implementation details.

principles.title: Narrative cadence
principles.description: The page alternates between dense information and breathing room so the reading experience feels guided rather than exhausting.

principles.title: Visible ownership
principles.description: Role, timeline, stack, and measurable outcomes need to be easy to find because they establish the scale of contribution.

principles.title: Code and craft together
principles.description: Implementation detail should feel native to the page instead of tacked on as an afterthought beneath the design story.

gallery.title: Case-study storytelling
gallery.caption: The screen balances structure and warmth so technical detail feels readable, not clinical.

case-study-storytelling.reflection: This study keeps reminding me that storytelling is also a layout problem. The same content can either feel heavy or readable depending on sequence, hierarchy, and pacing.

writing-journal-surface.label: Portfolio Redesign / Writing
writing-journal-surface.cardCaption: A reading surface for technical essays, code demos, and the quieter side of craft-building.

hero.title: Designing a writing surface for technical notes, code, and demos.
hero.description: This direction explores a quieter interface for articles about design systems, motion, accessibility, modern CSS, and frontend architecture.

writing-journal-surface.summary: A writing direction where technical notes, code examples, and interactive demos can live inside one consistent editorial system.
overview: The writing section needs to do more than list article titles. It should create enough calm and structure that readers want to stay with an idea, especially when the article includes code, live components, or implementation breakdowns.
This study focuses on readability, contrast, and modular content zones so that future posts can vary in format without breaking the tone of the portfolio.

principles.title: Typography-led reading
principles.description: The surface should let type, spacing, and rhythm do the heavy lifting instead of leaning on decorative layout noise.

principles.title: Interactive teaching space
principles.description: The layout anticipates code blocks and embedded component demos as first-class citizens of the article experience.

principles.title: Shared system, different mood
principles.description: The writing area should feel quieter than the project pages while still belonging unmistakably to the same design language.

gallery.title: Writing journal surface
gallery.caption: A calmer, more typographic direction for educational content, annotated code, and component experiments.

writing-journal-surface.reflection: This study is reinforcing how much good reading surfaces depend on restraint. When the layout gets quieter, the ideas themselves have more room.

design-showcase-gallery.label: Portfolio Redesign / Designs
design-showcase-gallery.cardCaption: A gallery system for showing design studies clearly, with enough context to make the work legible.

hero.title: Designing a gallery that can show evolving design work with clarity and confidence.
hero.description: This direction explores how to present interface studies and Figma work without overselling them or underselling the thinking behind them.

design-showcase-gallery.summary: A gallery surface for showing design studies, preview images, short context, and direct links back to the source Figma files.
overview: The design side of my practice is newer than the engineering side, so the page needs framing that is confident, transparent, and scalable as more studies, prototypes, and experiments get added.
This study builds that framing through compact cards, clear captions, and a system that can scale as more Figma projects, prototypes, and interface experiments are added.

principles.title: Honest framing
principles.description: The copy and visual tone should acknowledge growth while still presenting the work with confidence and care.

principles.title: Preview before explanation
principles.description: Visitors should be able to see the design direction first, then choose how much context they want to read.

principles.title: Direct source access
principles.description: Each study should link back to its Figma source so the portfolio feels transparent and traceable.

gallery.title: Design showcase gallery
gallery.caption: A compact but expandable system for documenting Figma studies as the design body of work grows.

design-showcase-gallery.reflection: This study matters because it gives the design side of the portfolio an explicit place to live. It turns ongoing practice into something visible, reviewable, and worth building on.
